Enterprise Testing Platform · AI Agent Setup Flow · Case Study
My Role — End-to-End UX Ownership

From the first sticky note on a whiteboard to the last design QA pass before launch.

I owned every phase of this work — not as a consultant handed off at key gates, but as an embedded design lead shaping the product's direction, rationale, and craft throughout.

Phase 01
Research & Discovery
Contextual inquiry, stakeholder interviews, task analysis with QA engineers across 3 teams. Synthesised into insight clusters and a shared research wall.
Phase 02
Problem Framing & Strategy
Facilitated problem reframing. Wrote the problem statement, HMW questions, and design goals. Positioned AI maturity deliberately against enterprise constraints.
Phase 03
IA, Flows & Artefacts
Produced full HFI-CUA artefact set: personas, mind map, top tasks, task flows, user flows (before/after), IA, and annotated wireframes.
Phase 04
Design & Prototyping
Designed the complete AI-first experience from entry point through live cockpit. Built interactive prototypes for usability testing and stakeholder reviews.
Phase 05
Validation & Implementation
Ran comparative usability sessions (SUS 52→86). Embedded with engineering through build. Iterated on live signals post-launch.
Title
Senior UX Designer
Methods
Contextual Inquiry · Usability Testing · Task Analysis · Systems Mapping · AI Workflow Design
Collaborators
Product Manager, Engineering Lead, QA Stakeholders, 5 QA Engineers (research participants)
01Discovery · What We Heard in the Field

We sat with QA engineers
for two weeks. Here's what the walls looked like.

We ran contextual inquiry sessions with 5 QA engineers and 2 QA leads across two enterprise teams. We observed their actual workflows — not the polished version they'd describe in a survey — and captured everything raw onto a shared research wall.

Setup Pain
"I spend more time configuring the test environment than actually writing the test. It's backwards."
Setup Pain
"Every time I start a new sprint I'm basically doing the same 30-minute setup ritual again."
Environment Selection
"Finding the right device + browser combo is genuinely painful. I just guess and hope for the best."
Environment Selection
"No one told me which devices to prioritise. I learned it from a 2-year-old Slack thread."
Debugging
"Flaky tests with unclear failures are killing my confidence in the results. I don't know what to trust."
Debugging
"The logs are technically accurate but completely useless for understanding what actually broke."
Tool Fragmentation
"I have 4 different tools open at the same time and none of them talk to each other."
Cognitive Load
"By the time I've finished setting up, I've already lost the mental context for what I was actually testing."
← Research synthesis note: Setup friction + mental load = the real problem. Testing quality is a symptom, not the root cause. — NV, Week 2

"Why does it take me longer to set up a test than to actually run it?"

— QA Engineer, Team 2 · Contextual Inquiry Session · The question that reframed the entire product direction

02Problem Reframing · Shifting the Question

The research didn't just confirm a problem.
It completely changed the question.

Every team we spoke to was trying to solve the wrong thing — optimising the tools while the workflow itself was fundamentally broken. Our job wasn't to make the existing system faster. It was to replace its premise.

Before We Talked to Users
"How do we make the existing testing tools faster and less painful?"
→
After Two Weeks in the Field
"What if the system could understand intent and handle execution entirely?"
We ObservedWe Reframed It AsDesign Implication
Tool executes testsSystem understands intentReplace configuration UI with natural language goal input
Manual device/browser selectionAI-driven environment orchestrationAI selects and justifies the optimal matrix automatically
Engineers monitor runs manuallyAI handles execution, human supervisesShift UI from operator dashboard to supervisor cockpit
Fragmented, disconnected toolsUnified intent-to-outcome platformSingle entry point that collapses the entire workflow
Cryptic log outputs for debuggingPlain-language failure narrativesAI summarises what happened and why, not just what failed
03Problem Definition · Sharp and Strategic

Before we designed a single screen,
we wrote this on the wall.

A vague problem produces a vague solution. We spent time making our problem definition sharp enough that it would reject bad ideas automatically.

Problem Statement
QA teams struggle with manual, time-consuming, and fragmented testing workflows that erode velocity, confidence, and the ability to ship with quality.
How Might We
How might we empower QA engineers to define what they want to test — and let an intelligent system handle how it gets done?
Design Goal
Enable QA engineers to achieve comprehensive test coverage with minimal effort and maximum confidence — by shifting their role from operator to supervisor.
04User Personas · Who We Were Designing For

Two very different people.
One shared frustration.

Our research surfaced two distinct user archetypes with overlapping pain but different success criteria. Designing for both without alienating either was a central tension throughout.

RK
Rahul K.
QA Engineer · 6 years experience · Web & Mobile
Automation Scripts Cross-browser CI/CD
Goals
  • Run reliable tests fast without setup overhead
  • Understand exactly what failed and why
  • Reduce time spent on flaky tests
  • Ship with confidence in coverage
Frustrations
  • Configuration takes longer than the test itself
  • Device matrix decisions feel like guesswork
  • Logs don't tell a human-readable story
  • Too many tools, zero integration
Typical Behaviour
  • Rewrites the same device config from memory every sprint
  • Maintains a personal Notion doc of "which device combos don't break things"
"I just want reliable tests that actually run. The setup is a tax I didn't sign up to pay."
MS
Meera S.
QA Lead · 11 years experience · Team of 6
Release Planning Team Efficiency Coverage Metrics
Goals
  • Get confident coverage visibility before every release
  • Reduce team time on repetitive execution work
  • Predictable, auditable testing pipelines
  • Present quality metrics to product leadership
Frustrations
  • Can't get a clear picture of team test health
  • Engineers blocked by setup, not by testing complexity
  • Release confidence depends on tribal knowledge
  • No standardised workflow across the team
Strategic Concerns
  • How do I know we have enough coverage before we ship?
  • Why is every engineer running a slightly different process?
"I need confidence in quality and speed — not a tool that makes my team work harder."
05Mind Map · Opportunity Space

Mapping everything we knew
before we narrowed down.

Before jumping to solutions, we mapped the full opportunity space — users, pain points, goals, AI capabilities, and desired outcomes — so we could see what connected to what.

Opportunity Space Mind Map — synthesised from research findings
06Top Tasks · What Users Actually Need to Do

Six tasks. One unified flow.
Every interaction traces back to these.

Through task analysis we identified the six core jobs QA engineers need to accomplish. Every design decision in the product either enables one of these tasks or gets cut.

🎯
Task 01
Define test goal
📱
Task 02
Select application / URL
🖥️
Task 03
Choose scope (devices/browsers)
⚡
Task 04
Run tests (with AI Agent)
📊
Task 05
Analyse results & insights
📤
Task 06
Share & report findings
07Task Flow · Run a Test Using AI Agent

The task flow we designed for.
Step by step, decision by decision.

Task flows let us map the critical path before touching UI. This is Task 04 — "Run tests with AI Agent" — which is the product's defining interaction and the one that most dramatically differs from the old world.

Task Flow — Run Test with AI Agent · Critical Path
08User Flows · Before and After AI Transformation

The same job. Two completely
different journeys.

Mapping the before and after user flows side by side made the transformation viscerally clear — not just to us, but to every stakeholder we presented to. The complexity difference spoke for itself.

User Flow — BEFORE AI · Manual Workflow · Total Time: 2–5 Hours
User Flow — AFTER AI · AI-First Workflow · Total Time: 20–40 Minutes
09Design Walkthrough · The AI Agent Setup Flow

7 steps. Every decision
has a reason behind it.

The "Run with AI Agent" flow is the product's defining interaction — the moment where the entire research, framing, and design thinking becomes real. Here's every screen, annotated with the UX rationale behind each decision.

Steps
01 / 07

Goal

Define what you want to achieve

Human-driven intent input
The entire paradigm shift lives in this single step. Engineers write what they want to test in plain language — no scripting, no configuration syntax. Template options were added after research showed that 60% of users had similar recurring goals; offering them as starting points reduced blank-slate anxiety and time-to-first-input by ~40%.
Design decisions in this step
1
Natural language objective
Free-text input replaces configuration scripts — the engineer defines intent, not instructions.
2
Template shortcuts
Pre-seeded templates surface the 5 most common QA goals, validated directly from our research sessions with QA teams.
3
Progress bar 1/7
Always-visible progress indicator anchors users in the flow and reduces abandonment from uncertainty about how long setup takes.
02 / 07

Application

Choose the app build or URL

Human-driven selection
A persistent pain in the old workflow was uploading and versioning builds manually each time. Here, all available builds and live URLs are pre-populated from the app catalogue. The most recent build is pre-selected, matching the 'default to latest' mental model we confirmed in usability testing.
Design decisions in this step
1
Versioned build list
All app versions and URLs unified in one place — no manual upload needed. Build date and type shown inline for quick disambiguation.
2
Pre-selected latest build
MyApp v2.4.1 is pre-selected. Research showed engineers almost always test the latest build; defaulting to it removes a redundant decision.
3
Completed step ✓
Goal step marked complete in sidebar. Visual progress reinforces momentum and reduces the urge to abandon mid-flow.
03 / 07

Scope

AI auto-prioritises your environments

AI suggests · Human overrides
This is where AI delivers its highest-value action in the entire flow. The platform analyses the goal type, app category, and historical failure data to pre-select the optimal device and browser matrix. Every checkbox reflects an AI recommendation — but every checkbox is also editable. This 'AI suggests, human approves' model was the direct product of our AI maturity positioning at Level 3–4.
Design decisions in this step
1
AI pre-selection
Devices and browsers pre-checked based on goal type + historical failure patterns. Engineers skip 15–20 minutes of manual selection entirely.
2
Override always available
Every checkbox is editable. The override affordance isn't a safety net — it's the trust signal that makes delegation feel safe.
3
Contextual OS labels
iOS 18.2, Android 14, macOS shown inline — removes the secondary lookup engineers previously needed to verify OS compatibility.
04a / 07

Instructions

Describe steps or paste a script

Optional · User-controlled
This step is intentionally optional — and that optionality is itself a design decision. Research showed most engineers don't need custom instructions for standard regression runs. Making this step skippable without consequences keeps the flow lean for the majority, while preserving power for advanced users who need to inject specific test paths.
Design decisions in this step
1
Natural language steps
Engineers can describe test steps in plain English — the same way they'd explain them to a colleague. AI parses and executes from these.
2
Upload Script option
Existing Selenium or custom scripts can be uploaded directly — meeting engineers where they are without forcing a workflow change.
3
Generate with AI
AI can generate test instructions from the goal defined in Step 1, closing the loop between intent and execution path.
04b / 07

Instructions (Script Loaded)

Confirmation state after upload

Feedback state · Error prevention
Once a script is uploaded, the confirmation state clearly shows the filename, load success, and a 'Remove' escape hatch. This addresses a key finding from usability testing: engineers were anxious about whether their uploaded script had actually registered correctly. The success state removes that doubt immediately.
Design decisions in this step
1
Success confirmation
Green check + filename + 'Script loaded successfully' — three layers of confirmation reduce error anxiety identified in testing.
2
Remove affordance
Inline 'Remove' lets engineers swap the script without navigating away — reduces backtracking friction significantly.
3
Greyed instruction text
The text area is visually de-emphasised once a script is loaded, preventing accidental dual-input that confused users in early prototypes.
05 / 07

Settings

Tune parallelism and retry behaviour

Optional · Expert configuration
Validated defaults mean most engineers never change these settings. The parallel runs count defaults to 4 — a value derived from average team device allocation. But the settings are exposed transparently rather than hidden, because enterprise users need to know the parameters of what's running. Visibility builds trust even when control isn't exercised.
Design decisions in this step
1
Parallel runs = 4
Default derived from research on typical team device allocation. Shown prominently so engineers understand what's happening, not just that something is.
2
Retry on failure dropdown
Configurable retry logic — key for flaky test environments. Collapsed by default to reduce noise for users who don't need it.
3
Stop on first failure toggle
Critical for fast-fail CI/CD pipelines. QA leads specifically requested this after seeing an early prototype without it.
06 / 07

Review

Confirm everything before launch

AI transparency moment
This is the most important trust-building screen in the entire product. It was not in the original scope — it emerged from usability testing when we watched engineers hesitate at the final 'Run' button because they couldn't see what they'd configured. The Review screen makes the AI's complete execution plan legible in plain language. After adding it, task completion on the final step rose from 71% to 96%.
Design decisions in this step
1
Complete execution summary
Every decision summarised in one scannable view: goal, app, devices, browsers, parallelism, retry logic, estimated runtime.
2
Estimated runtime
~4 min (6 environments) — a specific, honest time estimate. Research showed vague 'this may take a while' messages were a top anxiety driver.
3
All steps ✓ in sidebar
Every step complete in the sidebar. This moment of visual completeness was described by multiple test participants as 'satisfying' and 'reassuring'.
07a / 07

Run — Ready State

All clear. Start when you're ready.

Human-initiated launch
The final launch is always human-initiated — never automatic. This is a deliberate Level 3 AI maturity decision: AI has done all the heavy lifting, but the final 'go' belongs to the engineer. The green check + 'Ready to run' confirmation gives a clear moment of psychological closure before delegating execution to AI.
Design decisions in this step
1
Human-initiated launch
The engineer presses 'Start AI Test Run'. AI has prepared everything — but the final trigger is always human. Trust through control.
2
Ready to run state
Green confirmation icon + plain-language confirmation. Removes last-second doubt that was a top drop-off point in the original workflow.
3
Progress bar at 7/7
Complete progress bar. Visually communicates that setup is done — the cognitive load has been transferred to the system.
07b / 07

Run — AI Launching

AI Agent is taking over

AI execution state
Once launched, the AI provides immediate feedback that it's working — not a blank loading screen. The message 'Selecting available devices and prioritising environments based on your configuration' is specific and reassuring. It tells the engineer exactly what the AI is doing, maintaining the transparency principle that runs through every step of this flow.
Design decisions in this step
1
Specific loading message
Not 'Loading...' — but exactly what the AI is doing. Specificity maintains trust during the handover from human to autonomous execution.
2
Consistent sidebar ✓
All steps remain checked in the sidebar during execution. Engineers can see the complete configuration without needing to scroll back.
3
Spinner vs progress bar
Indeterminate spinner chosen over a progress bar because AI execution time is variable — a false progress bar was worse than honest uncertainty in user testing.
Step 1 of 9
10The Solution · AI Agent Interaction Design

One entry point.
Seven deliberate steps.

The entire experience collapses into a single button: "Run with AI Agent." What follows is a progressive stepper designed around the insight that every step must be either a meaningful human decision or a clearly-attributed AI action — never ambiguous.

1
Define the Goal
Natural language input. Engineers describe intent, not instructions. This single text field is the paradigm shift made tangible — the UI embodies the product's core premise.
User Input
2
Select Application
Unified app catalogue with automatic versioning and build management. No manual uploads, no drift between test environments and live builds.
User Input
3
AI Scope Suggestion
AI recommends the optimal device and browser matrix based on goal type, app category, and historical failure patterns. Every suggestion is explained in plain language. Override always available.
AI Suggests
4
Optional Instructions
Advanced users can inject scripts, constraints, or edge-case conditions. AI adapts its execution plan accordingly. Power without mandatory complexity for those who don't need it.
Optional
5
Parallelism & Retry Settings
Validated defaults mean most engineers never touch this. Those who do get precise control over concurrency, retry behaviour, and failure thresholds.
User Config
6
AI Execution Plan Review
The most important trust-building moment in the product. AI presents a human-readable summary of what it intends to do before running anything. Users see the why, not just the what.
AI Decision
7
Execution & Live Monitoring
AI runs the full suite. A live control cockpit provides real-time viewport, logs, screenshots, and network traces. Oversight without burden — the supervisor, not the operator.
AI Executes
11AI Maturity Spectrum · Deliberate Positioning

We could have built Level 5.
We chose Level 3–4 on purpose.

Enterprise products live or die on trust. Fully autonomous AI — however technically impressive — would have been rejected by our users' organisations. The sweet spot was supervised and monitored AI: enough autonomy to transform the workflow, enough control to earn institutional confidence.

Level 01
Human-Led
Human does everything. AI is absent.
Level 02
AI-Assisted
AI helps, suggests. Human executes.
Level 03 · Active
Supervised AI
AI performs, human approves each step.
Level 04 · Active
Monitored AI
AI operates. Human monitors & overrides.
Level 05
Autonomous AI
Fully independent. Human not needed.
We operate in Level 3–4. Enterprise trust, audit trails, and human override capability aren't constraints — they're design requirements that shaped every interaction decision in the product.
12Validation · Testing With Real QA Teams

We didn't just test if people
could use it. We tested if it changed behaviour.

We ran comparative usability sessions with 5 QA engineers and 2 QA leads — testing both the old workflow and the new prototype. We measured task time, error rates, confidence scores, and SUS — but the most valuable data was observational.

Participants
7
5 QA Engineers · 2 QA Leads across 3 enterprise teams
SUS Score Shift
52 → 86
From "Poor" to "Excellent" on the System Usability Scale
Overall Sentiment
4.6 / 5
Post-session satisfaction rating across all participants
🔍
Setup friction dominated — engineers spent 60%+ of pre-test time on configuration
Timing data from sessions confirmed our hypothesis: configuration was the single largest time sink, and it was entirely disconnected from the quality of the test itself. This validated AI-assisted environment selection as the highest-value intervention.
🌐
Device matrix selection relied on informal tribal knowledge — not system guidance
Engineers couldn't articulate why they chose specific device combos. "It works" and "someone told me" were the most common answers. This made the AI recommendation layer — with transparent rationale — immediately credible in testing.
📝
The AI execution plan review was the moment trust crystallised
When participants saw the plain-language "here's what I'll do and why" summary before execution, their body language visibly changed. Several leaned back. One said "oh, this is actually going to work." The transparency step was non-negotiable after this.
"If AI can handle setup, that alone saves me an hour. Easily. Every single time."
— QA Engineer · Validation Session 1
"The summary is what I needed all along. Stop making me read the logs."
— Senior QA Lead · Validation Session 3
"AI suggestions saved me so much time — and I could still change anything I wanted."
— QA Engineer · Validation Session 4
"Results are so much easier to understand now. I can actually explain what happened to my manager."
— QA Lead · Validation Session 6
13Artefact Map · How Everything Connected

Every artefact existed to inform
the one that followed it.

HFI-CUA methodology requires that artefacts aren't just produced — they actively shape each other. Here's the chain of influence that ran from first interview to final validation.

UX Artefact Connectivity Map — showing how each informed the next
14AI Design Principles · The Rules We Designed By

These weren't values on a wall.
They were decision filters.

Every time we faced a design fork — should AI do this automatically or surface it for human review? — we ran it through these principles. If a feature couldn't justify itself against all six, it didn't ship.

🎯
User Value First
AI for its own sake was cut without exception. Every autonomous feature had to demonstrably reduce engineer effort or cognitive load to earn its place in the product.
⚖️
Automation vs Augmentation
We mapped explicit ownership: what AI should own completely, and what requires human agency. Blurring this line was treated as a design defect, not a feature.
🔍
Radical Transparency
Every AI decision surfaces a plain-language rationale. Not in a log, not in a tooltip — right in the primary flow, before the action executes. Trust is built by making AI legible.
🕹️
Override Always Present
Control is made optional, never removed. Engineers delegate to AI because they know they can take it back. The override option isn't a safety net — it's a trust signal.
🛡️
Graceful Failure
Failure is a designed state, not an edge case. When AI fails, the system falls back predictably, communicates clearly, and never leaves the user in an ambiguous or unrecoverable state.
🏢
Enterprise Trust by Default
Audit trails, role-based access, and compliance-friendly reporting were architectural decisions made in Week 1 — not features added at the end to satisfy a checkbox.
85%
Reduction in
Setup Time
60%
Faster
Test Cycles
40%
Fewer
Manual Retries
50%
Cognitive
Load Reduced
15What's Next · The Foundation We Built For

Version one isn't the end.
It's the launchpad.

The platform was designed to evolve. Every architectural and interaction decision was made with the next horizon in mind — systems that don't just run tests, but learn from them.

01
Self-Healing Test Systems
Scripts that automatically adapt to UI changes, eliminating the maintenance overhead that consumes up to 30% of QA time across enterprise teams.
02
Predictive Failure Detection
AI learns from historical failure patterns to predict and surface risks before they reach production. QA shifts from reactive gate to proactive quality signal.
03
Fully Autonomous QA Pipelines
End-to-end test generation, execution, triage, and reporting — triggered by commits with zero human involvement unless escalation is genuinely required.

Systems that operate
on behalf of users.

This project demonstrates how AI transforms enterprise tools from configuration-heavy systems into intent-driven experiences — shifting users from operators to decision-makers, and from script writers to goal setters.
